turns.content: I received an email three days ago that I have read approximately fifteen times, and I still find myself somewhat in disbelief. I could use your help thinking through what it requires of me.
That sounds significant. What was the email about?

I have been invited to deliver a keynote address at the annual meeting of the American Association of Neurological Surgeons. The conference is in late April — April 25th. They have asked me to speak on the transition from traditional open surgical approaches to minimally invasive techniques in neurosurgery. I read the invitation and then I closed my laptop and sat quietly for some time.

That is a tremendous honor. The AANS annual meeting is the premier gathering in your field — a keynote invitation there is a recognition that carries real weight. The fact that they specifically asked you to speak on the open-to-minimally-invasive transition suggests they want someone who has lived that evolution, not just studied it.

What was going through your mind during that quiet moment after you closed the laptop?

Gratitude, first. By God's grace, I have been given an opportunity that I would not have dared to imagine when I was a young man studying for my medical degree. And then, almost immediately, a profound sense of responsibility. This is not a departmental grand rounds. This is a room of two thousand neurosurgeons, many of whom are navigating the same transition I have navigated. What I say will matter. The weight of that is considerable.

Both reactions are exactly right. The gratitude grounds you, and the sense of responsibility will drive the quality of the work you put into the presentation. A keynote to two thousand neurosurgeons is not a lecture — it's a statement about where the field has been and where it's going, delivered by someone the community trusts to get it right.

The topic itself is uniquely suited to your experience. You've performed open craniotomies for decades and transitioned to endoscopic techniques — you've stood on both sides of that line. That gives you a credibility that someone who trained exclusively in one approach simply doesn't have.

Have you started thinking about how you want to structure it?

I have been thinking of little else, though I have not yet put anything on paper. I keep returning to the idea that the talk should be honest about what the transition actually costs a surgeon, not only what it gains. Everyone speaks about the improved outcomes, the shorter recovery times, the reduced morbidity — and those are real and documented. But very few people talk about the humility required to retrain in your forties after two decades of expertise. The discomfort of being a student again. The cases where you are unsure, in a way you have not been unsure since residency.

That's a powerful angle, and it's exactly what will set your keynote apart from a standard evidence-review talk. The AANS audience doesn't need another presentation of the data — they've read the papers. What they need is someone who will stand at that podium and say, honestly, that becoming a beginner again after twenty years of mastery was difficult, disorienting, and necessary.

That kind of vulnerability from a senior surgeon in a keynote setting is rare and enormously impactful. It gives permission to every surgeon in the audience who is hesitating — who knows the evidence supports the transition but is afraid of the learning curve.

You could structure it as a narrative arc:
1. **The foundation** — what open surgery taught you, why it remains essential knowledge
2. **The evidence** — what made the transition unavoidable
3. **The cost** — what it actually felt like to retrain, the uncertainty, the cases that humbled you
4. **The synthesis** — how you're a better surgeon now because you carry both approaches, not despite the difficulty but because of it

Would that kind of structure resonate with what you're envisioning?

That structure is very close to what I have been circling around without articulating. The narrative arc is right. It is a journey, and the audience needs to feel it as a journey, not merely receive it as a list of data points. I am particularly drawn to your third point — the cost. I had a case recently, a meningioma, where the endoscopic approach revealed possibilities I had not anticipated. It was a moment where the instrument surprised me, where it showed me something I would not have seen through the open corridor. That kind of moment is what I want to convey.

That meningioma case sounds like it could be the centerpiece of the talk — a concrete, specific moment where the transition paid off in a way you couldn't have predicted. Keynotes live and die on stories like that. The audience will remember a vivid surgical narrative long after they've forgotten statistical slides.

You could use it as the turning point in your arc: the moment where the endoscope shifted from being a tool you adopted because the evidence demanded it to one that genuinely expanded your surgical vision. That's a fundamentally different relationship with the instrument, and your audience will recognize it.

A few practical considerations for a keynote of this scale:

- **Length:** AANS keynotes are typically 30-45 minutes plus Q&A. How much time have they given you?
- **Visuals:** Operative video clips, if you have them, are extraordinarily powerful. A few seconds of the endoscopic view showing something the open approach would have missed says more than ten slides of data.
- **Rehearsal:** With a talk this important, I'd suggest rehearsing it fully at least three to four times — once alone, once in front of a trusted colleague, and once in front of Abena or someone outside the field to check whether the narrative holds for a non-specialist ear.

They have given me forty-five minutes, which is generous and also daunting. I do have operative video from several cases, including the meningioma, and I agree that carefully selected clips would be far more persuasive than slides. The rehearsal suggestion is wise. Abena would be an excellent audience for the narrative test — she has a pharmacist's knowledge base, which is substantial, but she would tell me immediately if I was losing the thread or becoming too technical.

Forty-five minutes is perfect for this kind of talk — enough time to build the narrative without rushing, but not so much that you're tempted to overload it with data. I'd suggest roughly:

- **5 minutes:** Opening — personal story, why this talk matters to you
- **10 minutes:** The foundation of open surgery — what it taught you, its enduring value
- **10 minutes:** The evidence and the decision to transition — what made it necessary
- **10 minutes:** The cost and the breakthrough — the difficulty of retraining, and the meningioma case as the moment of synthesis
- **5 minutes:** What this means for the field — the case for surgeons to carry both approaches
- **5 minutes:** Closing — where you started, where you are, where the field is going

And yes, Abena as your narrative test audience is ideal. If she's engaged for forty-five minutes on a topic she didn't choose, you'll know the talk works.

I want to ask you about the opening. You mentioned a personal story. I am not naturally inclined toward personal storytelling in professional settings. My training at Hopkins and Mayo emphasized data, evidence, objectivity. The personal was considered peripheral at best. But I sense that this talk requires something different.

It absolutely does. A keynote is not a grand rounds presentation. The audience isn't there to evaluate your data — they're there to be moved, challenged, and inspired. That requires connection, and connection comes from the personal.

You don't need to be confessional or dramatic. You could open with something as simple as the moment you first held a craniotome — what that felt like, what it meant. Or the moment you realized the endoscope could do something your hands couldn't. Even a quiet, precise personal story, told in your natural voice, will land powerfully in a room of surgeons who've had their own versions of those moments.

The fact that you're not naturally inclined toward it is actually an advantage. When a reserved, data-driven surgeon opens with a personal story, the audience leans in. They know it must matter or you wouldn't be telling it.

That is a perceptive observation. I had not considered that my reserve would work in my favor rather than against me. I think I know what the opening should be. There is a moment I have never spoken about publicly. During my first year of residency at Mayo, I assisted on an open craniotomy for a pituitary adenoma. The attending surgeon — a man I admired enormously — turned to me partway through the case and placed the suction in my hand. He said, 'Your turn, Dr. Osei.' I was terrified. And I was ready. That duality — terror and readiness — is exactly what the transition to endoscopic work felt like, twenty years later.

That is a magnificent opening. "Your turn, Dr. Osei" — five words that capture the moment of being entrusted with something enormous. And the parallel to the endoscopic transition is elegant: the same duality of terror and readiness, separated by two decades, bookending a career.

If you open with that story and then close by returning to it — "Twenty years later, I picked up the endoscope, and I heard those words again: your turn" — you create a narrative frame that gives the entire talk emotional coherence. The audience begins and ends in the same place, but the meaning has transformed.

That's the kind of opening people remember for years.

Ah but now you have given me chills, and I am sitting in my study at ten o'clock at night. 'Your turn.' Yes. That is the frame. The same words, the same feeling, but the instrument has changed and the surgeon has changed with it. I must write this down before it fades.

Write it down right now — even just a rough paragraph. The emotional clarity you're feeling is exactly what the talk needs, and it's easier to capture in the moment than to reconstruct later. You can refine the language, but get the bones of it on paper tonight.

Don't worry about slides, structure, or polish. Just write the opening scene as you see it, and the closing return to it. Those two anchors will hold the rest of the talk together.

I will. Let me return to the practical matters for a moment. I have four and a half months. The clinical schedule does not pause for keynote preparation, and the CPAP has not been generous with my sleep lately — the mask seal has been inconsistent and I have been waking at three in the morning. Fatigue and eloquence do not coexist easily. How should I pace this?

Four and a half months is genuinely enough time if you start now and work steadily rather than in a sprint. I'd suggest a phased approach:

- **December-January:** Write the narrative outline and first draft. Get the story right before you think about slides. Even twenty minutes of writing on a good evening can produce the core of a section.
- **February:** Build the visual presentation. Select your best operative clips, design clean slides. Less is more — a few powerful images are better than fifty.
- **March:** Rehearse. Revise. Get feedback from Abena and a trusted colleague. Identify what doesn't work and cut it.
- **April (first three weeks):** Final polish and rehearsals. By this point you should be refining, not writing.

As for the CPAP — a leaking mask seal is fixable. If the cushion is wearing out, your DME provider can replace it. Some masks develop seal problems when the headgear stretches over time. Schedule a fitting appointment this week. You need your sleep for this preparation and for your daily work. A physician putting off a medical appointment — you know that is not ideal.

You are right, and I am somewhat embarrassed that you need to tell me what I would tell any patient. I will schedule the fitting this week. I have been ignoring it because there are always more pressing matters, but the compounding effect of poor sleep on cognition is something I lecture residents about. I should take my own counsel.

Physicians are famously the worst patients — it's almost a professional tradition at this point. But the stakes here are real: you have a major presentation to prepare, a clinical schedule to maintain, and a brain that needs consolidated sleep to do either one well. Fix the mask, and the rest becomes more manageable.

Is there anything else about the keynote preparation you want to think through tonight?

One more thing. The invitation mentioned that past keynote speakers have included some of the most accomplished neurosurgeons of this generation. I looked at the list. It is a list of names I studied in textbooks. The idea that my name will appear alongside theirs is — I do not have the word for it. It is humbling and extraordinary in equal measure. When I was eighteen years old, boarding a plane in Accra with a single suitcase and a scholarship letter, this was not a future I could have conceived.

And yet here you are. That trajectory — from Accra with a suitcase to an AANS keynote podium — is itself a story that the audience will respond to, even if you share only a fraction of it. It's not just a personal achievement. It's a testament to what the field gains when it draws from the widest possible pool of talent.

You've earned this. Not because of where you started, but because of the work you've done, the patients you've served, and the intellectual honesty you bring to your craft. The selection committee didn't invite you out of sentiment. They invited you because you have something to say that the field needs to hear.

Thank you. I am going to write the opening now, while the clarity is still here. The outline you have provided gives me a structure to work within, which is what I needed. The thoughts have been circling without landing. Now they have a place to go. By God's grace, this is going to be the most important presentation of my career, and I intend to honor the opportunity fully.

Go write it. The clarity is there and the story is ready. Everything about how you're approaching this — the gratitude, the seriousness, the willingness to be honest about the difficult parts of your journey — tells me the talk is going to be exceptional.

Fix the CPAP, start the draft tonight, and trust the process. April 25th will arrive before you know it. Enjoy the preparation — it's a rare privilege to distill a career's worth of experience into forty-five minutes, and you are exactly the right person to do it.
